stem:
A 27-year-old G1P0 at 40+3 in spontaneous labour has had an epidural sited two hours ago with a top-up given 25 minutes ago for a left-sided breakthrough. Her booking BMI was 24, she is a non-smoker, has no medical comorbidities, and the GBS swab at 36 weeks was negative. The last vaginal examination 40 minutes ago showed 8 cm dilatation, +0 station, direct OA, intact caput, no moulding.

Membranes ruptured spontaneously 6 hours ago with clear liquor. Oxytocin augmentation was started 90 minutes ago for slow progress between 6 and 8 cm and is currently at 12 mU/min with contractions 4 in 10, lasting 70 seconds, palpating moderate. The CTG over the last 30 minutes shows a baseline of 145 bpm, reduced variability of 3 to 5 bpm, no accelerations, and recurrent late decelerations occurring with more than 50% of contractions for the last 25 minutes.

There has been no prolonged deceleration. The mother is comfortable and the partogram shows progress consistent with primary dysfunctional labour. The obstetric registrar is at the bedside and theatre is available.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: 8 cm, +0, direct OA, clear liquor. Uterus soft between contractions. Palpates 4 in 10 moderate.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: baseline 145 bpm, variability 3-5 bpm, no accelerations, recurrent late decelerations >50% of contractions x 25 min. No tachysystole on tocodynamometer.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate next step in management?

options.text: Stop oxytocin, reposition to left lateral, give 500 mL IV crystalloid, perform foetal scalp lactate within 15 minutes
options.rationale: This CTG is FIGO pathological (recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability) but without a sustained bradycardia, so intrauterine resuscitation plus foetal scalp blood sampling for lactate is the appropriate next step before escalating to category 1 caesarean (RANZCOG IFSM 2019; FIGO 2015).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019
FIGO consensus guidelines on intrapartum foetal monitoring 2015

options.text: Continue oxytocin and observe for a further 30 minutes to determine if the pattern resolves spontaneously
options.rationale: Recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability for 25 minutes is pathological; continuing oxytocin worsens foetal acidosis. Observation is correct only with normal/suspicious traces (RANZCOG IFSM 2019).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

options.text: Proceed immediately to a category 1 caesarean section with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval
options.rationale: Category 1 CS is for acute decompensation (bradycardia, abruption, cord prolapse); pathological trace without acute decompensation warrants foetal scalp sampling first. Category 1 CS is correct with acute compromise (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously for acute tocolysis and continue oxytocin at the current rate
options.rationale: Terbutaline tocolysis is indicated for tachysystole-related compromise; no tachysystole here. Terbutaline is correct in tachysystole-driven decelerations (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

options.text: Perform an artificial rupture of membranes and apply a foetal scalp electrode without further intrauterine resuscitation
options.rationale: Membranes already ruptured and FSE without addressing the pathological pattern is insufficient. FSE alone is correct only with maternal-foetal CTG confusion needing direct trace (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019

explanation.summary: A FIGO pathological CTG without acute decompensation is managed with intrauterine resuscitation (stop oxytocin, left lateral, IV fluid, exclude hypotension, exclude tachysystole, consider tocolysis) and foetal scalp lactate sampling. Lactate >=4.8 mmol/L mandates delivery; 4.2-4.7 repeat in 30 minutes; <4.2 reassuring.
key_points: FIGO pathological = >=2 abnormal features OR sinusoidal OR prolonged bradycardia >5 min
Reduced variability + recurrent late decelerations = pathological
Intrauterine resuscitation mnemonic: SPOILT (Stop oxytocin, Position, Oxygen [no longer routinely recommended], IV fluid, Lower BP causes, Tocolysis)
Foetal scalp lactate cut-offs: <4.2 normal, 4.2-4.7 borderline, >=4.8 abnormal
Category 1 CS target decision-to-delivery 30 minutes
explanation.pearls: Reduced variability is the most predictive single feature of foetal acidosis. Recurrent late decelerations with a flat trace is the pattern that should provoke action even without bradycardia.

sources.label: RANZCOG Intrapartum Foetal Surveillance Clinical Guideline (IFSM) 2019
FIGO consensus guidelines on intrapartum foetal monitoring 2015

subtopic_detail: Intrapartum CTG - recurrent late decelerations with reduced variability

stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 at 39+1 in spontaneous labour at 6 cm has an epidural in situ that was sited 90 minutes ago and has provided good analgesia until the last hour, when she began to feel a sharp left-sided sensation with contractions. Her booking BMI was 27, she has no cardiac, respiratory or neurological comorbidities, no allergies, no anaphylaxis history, and her booking bloods including FBC and group and screen were unremarkable.

Five minutes ago the anaesthetic registrar gave a top-up of 10 mL of 0.1% ropivacaine with 2 microg/mL fentanyl for breakthrough pain. Her BP has now dropped from 122/74 to 84/46 mmHg with a maternal HR of 102. She feels light-headed but is conscious and is not nauseated, has no perioral numbness, no tinnitus and no metallic taste. The CTG shows a prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm that has now been ongoing for 4 minutes after previously reassuring features (baseline 140, variability 10-15, accelerations present).

There has been no vaginal bleeding, the uterus is soft between contractions (no hypertonus), the liquor was clear at ARM 30 minutes earlier, and there is no concern for cord prolapse on bedside vaginal examination. The anaesthetic registrar is at the bedside and IV access is patent through a 16G cannula.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 84/46 mmHg, HR 102 bpm (maternal), T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Awake, light-headed. Uterus soft between contractions. No PV bleeding. Cord not palpable.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration to 80 bpm for 4 minutes following epidural top-up.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate pharmacological intervention?

options.text: Ephedrine 6 mg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Ephedrine is acceptable but contemporary obstetric anaesthesia favours phenylephrine because ephedrine crosses placenta and increases foetal acidaemia. Ephedrine is correct only when phenylephrine unavailable or maternal bradycardia (ANZCA).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

options.text: Metaraminol 0.5 mg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Metaraminol is a reasonable alpha-agonist alternative commonly used in Australia, but phenylephrine has the strongest evidence base. Metaraminol is correct as second-line alpha-agonist (ANZCA).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

options.text: Phenylephrine 100 microg IV bolus, repeat at 1-2 minute intervals as needed
options.rationale: Phenylephrine 50-100 microg IV bolus is the first-line vasopressor for neuraxial-induced maternal hypotension because it preserves uterine blood flow and reduces foetal acidaemia compared with ephedrine; combined with left uterine displacement and a fluid bolus this typically resolves the prolonged deceleration (ANZCA; SOAP consensus 2018).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia
SOAP Consensus Statement on hypotension management 2018

options.text: Adrenaline 10 microg IV bolus and prepare for category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: Adrenaline boluses are reserved for cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis; this is treatable neuraxial hypotension. Adrenaline is correct in cardiac arrest or anaphylaxis only (ANZCA).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

options.text: Atropine 600 microg IV bolus to correct the maternal heart rate
options.rationale: Maternal HR is 102; atropine treats bradycardia not hypotension and would not correct uteroplacental hypoperfusion. Atropine is correct in bradycardia-driven hypotension only (ANZCA).
source_refs: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia

explanation.summary: Prolonged deceleration following an epidural top-up is most commonly due to maternal hypotension reducing uteroplacental perfusion. Manage with full left lateral position, IV crystalloid bolus 250-500 mL, and phenylephrine 50-100 microg IV bolus (first-line). If the deceleration does not resolve within 9 minutes, prepare for category 1 caesarean.
key_points: Phenylephrine first-line for neuraxial hypotension in obstetrics
Ephedrine causes more foetal acidaemia (crosses placenta, beta-agonist on foetal heart)
Metaraminol is an acceptable Australian alternative
Always exclude cord prolapse, abruption and uterine rupture as causes of prolonged deceleration
9-minute rule: prolonged deceleration not recovering requires immediate delivery decision
explanation.pearls: Treat the maternal BP first; the foetal heart rate almost always follows. If you are reaching for atropine for the baby, you have the wrong drug.

sources.label: ANZCA Professional Document PS - Obstetric Anaesthesia
SOAP Consensus Statement on hypotension management 2018

subtopic_detail: Prolonged deceleration after epidural top-up - vasopressor choice

stem: A 28-year-old G1P0 at 40+1 in spontaneous labour, admitted 6 hours ago at 5 cm, is re-examined and is now 6 cm at +0 station in direct OP position with mild caput and no moulding. Membranes ruptured spontaneously 4 hours ago with clear liquor. Contractions: palpate 3 in 10 lasting 40 seconds, mild intensity. CTG normal. Hb 124. Her mother is reminding the registrar that her own labour had to be 'started with the drip' and asks them to do the same.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 116/74 mmHg, HR 86 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: 6 cm, +0, direct OP, mild caput, no moulding. Contractions 3 in 10, 40 s, mild.
data_table.Investigations: CTG normal. GBS negative. Hb 124 g/L.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 1-2 mU/min, titrated every 30 minutes to achieve 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes
options.rationale: Membranes are already ruptured and there is inadequate contraction frequency/strength causing slow progress in the active first stage. Oxytocin augmentation at 1-2 mU/min titrated every 30 minutes is the correct Australian protocol; faster titration risks hyperstimulation (RANZCOG; eTG).

options.text: Perform ARM and reassess in 4 hours before considering oxytocin
options.rationale: Membranes already spontaneously ruptured; ARM not applicable. ARM is correct in intact membranes with poor progress (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed to category 2 caesarean section for failure to progress
options.rationale: Failure to progress requires demonstration after adequate uterine activity (after oxytocin); proceeding to caesarean before oxytocin is premature. Cat 2 CS is correct after oxytocin trial fails (RANZCOG).

options.text: Commence IV oxytocin infusion starting at 6 mU/min with no titration interval shorter than 60 minutes
options.rationale: Starting at 6 mU/min risks hyperstimulation and foetal compromise; standard starts at 1-2 mU/min titrated every 30 minutes. High-dose start is correct only with maximum-dose review and continuous foetal monitoring in selected protocols (RANZCOG).

options.text: Site an epidural and re-examine her in 6 hours without commencing any augmentation now
options.rationale: Pain relief is reasonable but does not address slow progress with inadequate contractions; delay risks foetal compromise. Epidural without augmentation is correct when contractions are adequate (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Primary dysfunctional labour in a nullipara at 6 cm with inadequate contractions and intact membranes is managed with oxytocin augmentation (after ARM if membranes intact). Start at 1-2 mU/min and double every 30 minutes to a usual maximum of 32 mU/min, titrated to 4-5 contractions per 10 minutes. Diagnose labour dystocia only after at least 4 hours of adequate contractions with no progress, then proceed to CS.
key_points: Active phase: >=6 cm with regular contractions (Zhang/ACOG)
Adequate progress nullipara: ~0.5-1 cm/h after 6 cm
Oxytocin start 1-2 mU/min, double every 30 min, max ~32 mU/min
Target: 4-5 contractions per 10 min, 45-60 s, moderate-strong
Diagnose dystocia after >=4 h adequate contractions without progress
explanation.pearls: Direct OP at 6 cm is a common cause of slow progress; many will rotate spontaneously with adequate contractions plus mobility/epidural.

sources.label: KEMH Clinical Guideline: Augmentation of labour
Royal Women's Hospital - Oxytocin in labour

subtopic_detail: Failure to progress in active first stage - oxytocin augmentation

stem:
A 28-year-old G1P0 at 38+2 in spontaneous labour at 10 cm dilatation has just had a spontaneous rupture of membranes with a large gush of clear liquor. The midwife performing a vaginal examination immediately feels a pulsating loop of cord in the vagina at +0 station. The foetal head is at -2 station and direct OA, with 3/5 of the head still palpable abdominally.

The CTG was previously reassuring (baseline 140, variability 10, accelerations present, no decelerations) but now shows a prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm starting within the last minute. The mother is comfortable on her right side with an epidural in situ; her observations are BP 122/78, HR 88, T 36.9, SpO2 99%. She is otherwise well, has no obstetric or medical comorbidities, and her 36-week growth scan was unremarkable.

The emergency buzzer has been pulled and the obstetric, anaesthetic and neonatal teams are en route. She has had no previous abdominal surgery, no caesarean section, and is otherwise low risk for surgical complications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 122/78 mmHg, HR 88 bpm, T 36.9 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: 10 cm, -2 station, direct OA, pulsating cord at +0 in vagina. Liquor clear. Bladder catheterised.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration to 70 bpm, starting within the last minute.

lead_in: Which immediate set of actions is most appropriate?

options.text: Reduce the cord back into the uterus through the cervix and proceed to instrumental vaginal delivery in the room
options.rationale: Cord replacement is no longer recommended due to cord trauma and vasospasm; manual elevation of presenting part is preferred. Cord replacement is essentially obsolete (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

options.text: Continue oxytocin augmentation, reposition the mother to left lateral and reassess CTG again in 5 minutes
options.rationale: Oxytocin must be STOPPED immediately to reduce contractions compressing cord; 5-minute observation is unsafe with prolonged deceleration. Continued oxytocin is essentially never correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

options.text: Administer terbutaline 250 microg subcutaneously and proceed immediately to assisted vaginal birth using a ventouse now
options.rationale: Tocolysis with terbutaline may be considered for hyperstimulation or transfer delay, but ventouse at -2 station is contraindicated (station must be >=+2). Tocolysis + ventouse combination is never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Manually elevate the presenting part off the cord, knee-chest or exaggerated Sims, fill bladder 500 mL if delay, category 1 caesarean
options.rationale: At -2 station vaginal delivery is not imminent. Manual elevation of the presenting part off the cord (or bladder filling with 500-700 mL warmed saline if transfer delay) plus knee-chest/Sims position plus category 1 CS within 30 minutes is the standard pathway (RANZCOG; RCOG Green-top 50).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

options.text: Wait for the next contraction at full dilatation and perform a fundal pressure delivery to expedite vaginal birth
options.rationale: Fundal pressure contraindicated; head is at -2 station with vaginal delivery not feasible. Category 1 CS required. Pushing-fundal pressure is essentially never correct in cord prolapse (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Cord prolapse with a non-imminent vaginal delivery requires manual elevation of the presenting part (or bladder filling with 500-700 mL warmed saline for transfer delay), knee-chest or exaggerated Sims position, stop oxytocin, consider tocolysis if hyperstimulation, and category 1 caesarean section with a 30-minute decision-to-delivery interval.
key_points: Diagnosis: pulsating cord palpated below the presenting part
Manual elevation preferred over cord replacement
Bladder filling 500-700 mL saline if transfer delay
Stop oxytocin; consider terbutaline 250 microg SC if hyperstimulation
Category 1 CS, decision-to-delivery target 30 min (RANZCOG/NICE)
Instrumental vaginal birth only if fully dilated AND station >=+2 AND operator confident
explanation.pearls: Keep the examiner's hand in the vagina elevating the presenting part during transfer; do not handle the cord more than necessary because handling causes vasospasm.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 50: Umbilical cord prolapse

subtopic_detail: Cord prolapse - immediate management at full dilatation

stem:
A 24-year-old G1P0 at 40+0 with an epidural is in active second stage. She has been pushing for 110 minutes without descent of the presenting part beyond +1 station. The foetal head is at +1, direct OA, with no caput or moulding palpable, and 0/5 of the head is palpable abdominally on Crichton assessment. The maternal pelvis was clinically adequate on assessment by both registrar and consultant.

The CTG shows a baseline of 145 with variability 5-10 bpm, no accelerations and variable decelerations with most contractions but no late or prolonged decelerations and no sinusoidal pattern. Membranes are ruptured with clear liquor of normal volume. Contractions are 4 in 10 with oxytocin running at 16 mU/min. The mother is exhausted, asking for help to deliver, and has had a thorough antenatal discussion of birth options including operative delivery.

The bladder is empty after recent catheter in/out. Adequate epidural top-up has been given and the block is dense to ice. The neonatal team has been notified.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 118/72 mmHg, HR 96 bpm, T 37.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: +1 station, direct OA, 0/5 head palpable abdominally, no caput, no moulding. Bladder empty. Epidural adequate.
data_table.Investigations: CTG suspicious (variable decelerations, reduced variability), not pathological.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate instrument and approach for this delivery?

options.text: Kielland's rotational forceps for a deep transverse arrest under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Position is direct OA, not transverse arrest; Kielland's restricted to experienced operators for rotational deliveries. Kielland's is correct in deep transverse arrest by experienced operators (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

options.text: Low-cavity non-rotational outlet forceps (Neville-Barnes or Simpson) under regional anaesthesia
options.rationale: Direct OA at +1 station with maternal exhaustion and adequate epidural is suitable for low-cavity non-rotational forceps. Forceps have a lower failure rate than ventouse in maternal exhaustion and a suspicious (but not pathological) CTG (RANZCOG; RCOG GTG 26).
source_refs: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

options.text: Sequential ventouse then forceps if the initial vacuum extraction attempt fails to descend
options.rationale: Sequential instrument use significantly increases neonatal morbidity (ICH, subgaleal); generally avoided. Sequential is correct only when head on perineum and primary instrument fails (RCOG).

options.text: Kiwi OmniCup soft ventouse with traction synchronous with maternal contractions only and pulls
options.rationale: Ventouse has higher failure rate than forceps in maternal exhaustion and suspicious CTG, plus higher cephalohaematoma/subgaleal rates. Ventouse is correct in early descent and adequate maternal effort (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to a category 2 caesarean section now without attempted instrumental delivery
options.rationale: At +1 station with 0/5 palpable abdominally and direct OA, trial of instrumental is appropriate. Category 2 CS is correct after failed instrumental or with malposition not amenable to instruments (RANZCOG; NICE).

explanation.summary: For mid-cavity or low-cavity OA position with maternal exhaustion and a suspicious (not pathological) CTG, non-rotational forceps have a higher success rate than ventouse and a lower neonatal cephalohaematoma rate, but a higher rate of maternal perineal trauma. Sequential instrument use is associated with higher neonatal morbidity and should be avoided. Document foetal station, position, caput/moulding, bladder, epidural, and consent prior to commencing.
key_points: Pre-instrumental checklist: full dilatation, ruptured membranes, head <=1/5 palpable abdominally, defined station, defined position, adequate analgesia, empty bladder, consent
Forceps: higher success rate, more maternal trauma
Ventouse: higher failure rate, more cephalohaematoma/subgaleal haemorrhage
Sequential use significantly increases neonatal morbidity
Failed instrumental: stop after 3 contractions with no descent or after 20 minutes total
explanation.pearls: Choose the instrument most likely to succeed first. If you would normally choose ventouse but conditions are marginal (exhaustion, suspicious CTG, OP), forceps usually has the better outcome.

sources.label: RCOG Green-top Guideline 26: Assisted Vaginal Birth

subtopic_detail: Instrumental birth - forceps vs ventouse selection

stem: A 30-year-old G2P1 at 39+4 having a trial of labour after caesarean (one previous LSCS for breech) is in active labour at 7 cm with epidural. The CTG develops a sudden prolonged deceleration to 60 bpm, not recovering after 4 minutes despite stopping oxytocin, left lateral, 500 mL crystalloid and exclusion of hypotension. Fresh PV bleeding develops, scar tenderness, and the previously palpable head becomes ballotable abdominally (suspected uterine rupture). The registrar calls a category 1 caesarean section.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 102/64 mmHg, HR 116 bpm, T 36.7 degC.
data_table.Examination: Scar tender, fresh PV bleeding, head ballotable abdominally.
data_table.Investigations: CTG: prolonged deceleration 60 bpm, not recovering at 4 minutes.

lead_in: Which combination most accurately describes the standards for category 1 caesarean and the immediate priorities?

options.text: Decision-to-delivery interval target 30 minutes; concurrent MTP activation, theatre transfer, anaesthetic preparation; do not delay for re-examination
options.rationale: The Australian/NICE standard for category 1 caesarean is delivery as soon as possible, with a 30-minute auditable threshold; bypassing further bedside investigation is appropriate when uterine rupture is suspected, and parallel resuscitation and MTP activation are essential (RANZCOG; RCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 15 minutes; defer MTP activation until intraoperative blood loss is confirmed
options.rationale: 15 minutes is idealised target rarely achieved; audited standard is 30 minutes. MTP should be activated proactively given suspected rupture. 15-minute target is correct only in immediate intra-theatre rupture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 60 minutes; obtain confirmatory bedside ultrasound before transfer to theatre
options.rationale: 60 minutes is threshold for category 2 CS; rupture is category 1 emergency. 60 minutes is correct for category 2 (RANZCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 75 minutes if epidural is already sited and theatre is in-house
options.rationale: Epidural availability does not change category-1 timing; target is 30 minutes. 75 minutes is essentially never appropriate in suspected rupture (RANZCOG).

options.text: Decision-to-delivery 45 minutes; abandon TOLAC pathway only after delivery is achieved
options.rationale: TOLAC is abandoned the moment rupture is suspected, not after delivery; target is 30 minutes. 45 minutes is appropriate only in category 2 status (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Categorisation of urgency for caesarean section (RANZCOG/NICE): Cat 1 - immediate threat (target 30 min decision-to-delivery); Cat 2 - maternal or foetal compromise, not immediately life-threatening (target 60-75 min); Cat 3 - early delivery needed but no compromise; Cat 4 - delivery timed to suit woman or staff.
key_points: Cat 1: 30 minutes (audit standard)
Cat 2: 60-75 minutes (audit standard)
30 minutes is an audit standard, not a strict outcome threshold
Common Cat 1 indications: cord prolapse, abruption, uterine rupture, scalp pH <7.20, prolonged bradycardia
explanation.pearls: Document time of decision, time of arrival in theatre, time of knife-to-skin, and time of delivery for every Cat 1 CS for departmental audit.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Categorisation of urgency for caesarean section

stem:
A 32-year-old G3P3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 40+2 of a 4.0 kg neonate after a 9-hour first stage and a 45-minute second stage with oxytocin augmentation for slow progress in the first stage. Active management of the third stage with IM oxytocin 10 units has been given and the placenta is delivered intact 8 minutes after the baby.

There is now ongoing brisk PV bleeding estimated at 1200 mL over 10 minutes with a soft, boggy uterus that is poorly contracted at the umbilicus. The bladder has been catheterised and is empty. Bimanual compression has been commenced. IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 250 mL/h is already running through a 16G cannula. Her observations are BP 96/58, HR 118, T 36.7.

She has a history of asthma (mild, intermittent, no recent exacerbations) but no hypertensive disorder of pregnancy and no other medical comorbidities. Blood has been sent for FBC, coagulation, fibrinogen and group and crossmatch.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 96/58 mmHg, HR 118 bpm, T 36.7 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Uterus soft and boggy, fundus at umbilicus. Bladder empty. No genital tract trauma visible. Placenta delivered intact.
data_table.Investigations: Estimated blood loss 1200 mL. FBC/coags/fibrinogen/X-match sent. Asthma history (mild, intermittent).

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate next pharmacological step in addition to ongoing IV oxytocin infusion?

options.text: PR misoprostol 800 microg with antiemetic precaution and continue the IV oxytocin infusion at current rate
options.rationale: Misoprostol is third-line in Australian guidance because of slower onset and higher pyrexia/shivering; ergometrine and carboprost are preferred when available and not contraindicated. Misoprostol is correct when ergometrine/carboprost contraindicated (RANZCOG PPH).

options.text: IM carboprost 250 microg, repeated every 15 minutes to a maximum of 8 doses in total
options.rationale: Carboprost is contraindicated in asthma; ergometrine is preferred second-line here. Carboprost is correct in non-asthmatic PPH (AMH; Therapeutic Guidelines).

options.text: IM ergometrine 250 microg plus IV tranexamic acid 1 g over 10 minutes
options.rationale: Atonic PPH with no contraindication to ergometrine (no hypertension/pre-eclampsia/cardiac disease): ergometrine 250 microg IM (or IV slowly) is second-line, plus IV TXA 1 g over 10 min within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial). Asthma is a relative contraindication to carboprost, not ergometrine (RANZCOG; Therapeutic Guidelines; AMH).

options.text: Increase IV oxytocin infusion to 60 units in 1 L Hartmann's at 500 mL/h and observe response for 15 minutes
options.rationale: Maximum oxytocin is 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's over 4 hours; doses above this risk water intoxication. Oxytocin escalation beyond max is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert an intrauterine balloon tamponade with 500 mL warmed saline immediately and withhold further uterotonics
options.rationale: Balloon tamponade is appropriate after failure of medical management; uterotonics and TXA should be optimised first. Balloon is correct after first/second-line uterotonic failure (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Atonic PPH stepwise: massage and compression, oxytocin (10 IU IM/IV bolus then 40 IU/1 L IV infusion), ergometrine 250 microg IM/IV (if no contraindication), carboprost 250 microg IM q15min up to 8 doses (avoid in asthma), misoprostol 800 microg PR if other agents unavailable. Give IV TXA 1 g over 10 min within 3 hours of birth. Escalate to balloon tamponade, B-Lynch, internal iliac ligation, hysterectomy.
key_points: Ergometrine contraindicated: hypertension, pre-eclampsia, cardiac disease, Raynaud's
Carboprost contraindicated: asthma (relative)
Misoprostol side effects: pyrexia, shivering
TXA 1 g IV within 3 hours of birth (WOMAN trial 2017)
Max IV oxytocin 40 units in 1 L Hartmann's over 4 hours
explanation.pearls: If hypertensive: avoid ergometrine, use carboprost (if no asthma). If asthma: avoid carboprost, use ergometrine (if no hypertension). If both: misoprostol.

subtopic_detail: Postpartum haemorrhage - second-line uterotonic after oxytocin

stem:
A 24-year-old G1P0 at 28+3 is admitted with a 24-hour history of severe frontal headache, blurred vision and right upper quadrant pain. She booked at 14 weeks with a BP of 116/72 and no proteinuria; her booking BMI was 23 and she has no chronic hypertension, no diabetes and no autoimmune disease. The 20-week morphology scan was normal and the most recent growth scan at 26 weeks showed an appropriately grown foetus with normal Dopplers.

Her BP on arrival is 178/116 mmHg confirmed on repeat with a manual cuff after 5 minutes' rest, with 3+ proteinuria on dipstick and a urine PCR of 380 mg/mmol. She is reflexively brisk at 3+ with no clonus, no focal neurology, and no visual field defect on confrontation. Bloods show platelets 92, ALT 184, urate 0.42, creatinine 88, LDH normal, no haemolysis on film, INR 1.0, fibrinogen 4.2.

A magnesium sulfate loading dose of 4 g IV over 20 minutes was given 30 minutes ago and a maintenance infusion of 1 g/h is now running. Despite the magnesium load her headache and blurred vision persist and her BP remains 174/112. The CTG is currently reassuring with a baseline of 138 and normal variability.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 174/112 mmHg, HR 92 bpm, T 36.8 degC, SpO2 99%.
data_table.Examination: Headache, blurred vision persist. Hyperreflexia 3+, no clonus. RUQ tender.
data_table.Investigations: Platelets 92 (x10^9/L), ALT 184 U/L, urate 0.42 mmol/L, creatinine 88 micromol/L, urine PCR 380 mg/mmol, no haemolysis. CTG reassuring.

lead_in: Which is the most appropriate immediate additional antihypertensive intervention?

options.text: IV hydralazine 5 mg bolus, then 5-10 mg every 20 minutes (max 30 mg) titrated to BP <160/110
options.rationale: Hydralazine is acceptable second- or third-line in Australian guidance, associated with more maternal hypotension and foetal distress than labetalol/nifedipine. Hydralazine is correct when labetalol/nifedipine fail or contraindicated (SOMANZ 2023).

options.text: IV labetalol 20 mg bolus, then 40 mg, then 80 mg every 10 minutes (max 220 mg) titrated to BP <160/110
options.rationale: Labetalol IV is first-line for acute severe hypertension in pre-eclampsia per SOMANZ 2023 (Australian guidance), with nifedipine immediate-release as an alternative first-line. Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis and does not lower BP. Delivery is indicated after stabilisation given persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium (SOMANZ).

options.text: Oral nifedipine immediate-release 10 mg, repeated every 30 minutes to a maximum of three doses for control
options.rationale: Oral immediate-release nifedipine is acceptable first-line alternative to IV labetalol per SOMANZ, but carries overshoot hypotension risk. PO nifedipine is correct when IV access difficult (SOMANZ).

options.text: Sublingual nifedipine 10 mg capsule bitten and swallowed, repeated every 20 minutes as required to a maximum of three doses
options.rationale: Sublingual nifedipine is contraindicated in pregnancy due to unpredictable absorption and severe overshoot hypotension. SL nifedipine is essentially never correct in pregnancy (SOMANZ).

options.text: Increase the magnesium sulfate maintenance infusion from 1 g/h to 2 g/h to achieve blood pressure control in her
options.rationale: Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis only and does not lower BP. Increased Mg risks toxicity. Mg dose change is correct for breakthrough seizure or sub-therapeutic levels (SOMANZ).

explanation.summary: Severe pre-eclampsia with persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium load requires (1) acute BP control to <160/110 with IV labetalol or oral nifedipine IR (first-line per SOMANZ), with hydralazine second-line, and (2) delivery after stabilisation (antenatal steroids if not given and time permits, but delivery should not be unduly delayed). Magnesium is for seizure prophylaxis, not BP control.
key_points: Severe HTN target: <160/110 within 30-60 minutes
SOMANZ first-line acute severe HTN: IV labetalol OR oral nifedipine IR
Hydralazine: second/third-line, more maternal hypotension
Magnesium: seizure prophylaxis only (4 g IV load, 1 g/h maintenance)
Persistent neurological symptoms despite magnesium = imminent eclampsia = deliver
Antenatal steroids 11.4 mg betamethasone IM x 2 doses 24 h apart if <34+6 weeks
explanation.pearls: Stabilise mother, then deliver. The cure for pre-eclampsia is delivery, and persistent neurological symptoms after a magnesium load are an indication that delivery cannot wait for the full steroid course.

subtopic_detail: Severe pre-eclampsia at 28 weeks - antihypertensive selection

stem:
A 31-year-old G2P1 with a dichorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy at 37+3 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth of Twin 1 (cephalic, 2.6 kg) with intact membranes of the second sac. She has an epidural in situ. After delivery of Twin 1 her uterus relaxes and bedside ultrasound now shows Twin 2 in a transverse lie with the back inferior and the membranes still intact.

The estimated foetal weight of Twin 2 at the 36-week scan was 2.8 kg. The CTG of Twin 2 is reassuring with a baseline of 140 bpm, variability 10-15, accelerations present, no decelerations. The cervix remains fully dilated. The obstetric consultant is in theatre attached to the room, and the anaesthetist confirms a working epidural with a dense block. There is no PV bleeding and the uterus is currently soft. The team has been briefed and the mother had a thorough antenatal discussion of intrapartum options for twin birth.

data_table.Examination: Cervix fully dilated. Twin 2 transverse, back inferior, membranes intact. Uterus relaxed.
data_table.Investigations: USS at bedside: transverse lie, back inferior. CTG reassuring.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management of Twin 2 at this point?

options.text: External cephalic version under tocolysis followed by ARM when cephalic and longitudinal
options.rationale: ECV for second twin has lower success and higher failure than internal podalic version with breech extraction; with back inferior and relaxed uterus, internal podalic version is preferred. ECV is correct with back-up presentation and superior position (RANZCOG).

options.text: ARM immediately to allow the head to descend by gravity
options.rationale: ARM with transverse lie is CONTRAINDICATED due to high cord prolapse risk; version must occur first. ARM-first in transverse lie is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Immediate emergency caesarean section for delivery of the second twin
options.rationale: Caesarean for second twin after vaginal birth of first is associated with higher maternal morbidity than internal podalic version with breech extraction in experienced hands. CS for twin 2 is correct only after failed manoeuvres (RANZCOG).

options.text: Wait 60 minutes for spontaneous version before any intervention
options.rationale: Delay risks cord prolapse on subsequent ROM, abruption from uterine retraction, and closing cervix; prompt management within 30 minutes is recommended. Waiting 60 minutes is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Internal podalic version with breech extraction performed under continued epidural anaesthesia
options.rationale: Transverse lie of the second twin with intact membranes, a relaxed uterus and a working epidural is the classic indication for internal podalic version with breech extraction by a skilled operator. This avoids caesarean for the second twin and outcomes are equivalent or better when performed by experienced operators (RANZCOG; ACOG).
source_refs: Twin Birth Study (Barrett et al. NEJM 2013)

explanation.summary: For a non-cephalic second twin after delivery of Twin 1, internal podalic version with breech extraction is the preferred approach by a skilled operator with a working epidural and a relaxed uterus. ECV is an alternative but has higher failure rates. Caesarean for the second twin is reserved for cord prolapse, foetal compromise unresponsive to manoeuvres, or operator inexperience.
key_points: Twin Birth Study (NEJM 2013): planned vaginal birth non-inferior to planned CS at >=32 weeks if Twin 1 cephalic
IPV preferred with intact membranes, relaxed uterus, working epidural
ECV alternative; higher failure rate
Always have theatre ready and the consultant available
Deliver Twin 2 within 30 minutes of Twin 1 to reduce abruption and cord prolapse risk
explanation.pearls: Do not break the membranes of Twin 2 until you have a cephalic or breech longitudinal lie. ROM with a transverse lie is a category 1 emergency.

sources.label: Twin Birth Study (Barrett et al. NEJM 2013)

subtopic_detail: Twin delivery - second twin with non-cephalic lie

stem:
A 36-year-old G4P3 at 39+4 has just had a spontaneous vaginal birth at 09:42 after a 4-hour labour augmented with oxytocin from 5 cm for slow progress, ending with a second stage of 30 minutes and an intact perineum. The placenta was delivered by controlled cord traction at 09:48 and active third-stage management with IM oxytocin 10 units had been given immediately after the birth.

Two minutes after delivery of the placenta she suddenly develops dyspnoea, agitation and a sense of impending doom while talking to her partner. Within 60 seconds she becomes cyanotic, loses consciousness and her monitor shows BP 60/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 78% on 15 L/min via Hudson mask, with the trace transitioning to a narrow-complex tachycardia. Within 90 seconds she becomes pulseless with a rhythm of PEA on the defibrillator.

There is now brisk PV bleeding from the uterus despite a soft contracted fundus on bimanual palpation, and oozing from her peripheral IV site and recent venipuncture. She has no history of cardiac disease, no epidural was sited at any point, no local anaesthetic was used apart from 5 mL of 1% lignocaine for a perineal infiltration after the birth, and there were no transfusions or blood products administered during labour. CPR is commenced with manual left uterine displacement and the obstetric, anaesthetic, ICU and haematology teams are paged through the MET emergency call.

data_table.Vital signs: Pre-arrest: BP 60/40 mmHg, HR 130 bpm, SpO2 78% on 15 L/min O2. Now pulseless with PEA.
data_table.Examination: Cyanotic, unconscious. Brisk PV bleeding from soft contracted uterus. Oozing from IV site.
data_table.Investigations: Bloods drawn: anticipate prolonged PT/APTT, low fibrinogen, low platelets. CTG no longer relevant.

lead_in: Which diagnosis best explains this presentation?

options.text: Amniotic fluid embolism with cardiopulmonary collapse and DIC
options.rationale: Sudden peripartum collapse with hypoxia, hypotension and coagulopathy (PPH plus bleeding from puncture sites) is the classic Clark triad of AFE. PEA arrest within minutes of placental delivery in a multipara fits the AFE phenotype; management is high-quality CPR with left uterine displacement, A-OK protocol (atropine, ondansetron, ketorolac), early massive transfusion and cardiac arrest team activation (RANZCOG; AFE Foundation; Clark 2016).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium
Clark SL et al. Proposed diagnostic criteria for AFE 2016

options.text: Local anaesthetic systemic toxicity (LAST) from perineal lignocaine infiltration
options.rationale: Only 5 mL of 1% lignocaine (50 mg) was used, far below toxic threshold (3 mg/kg without adrenaline); LAST presents with seizures and arrhythmia. LAST is correct with confirmed high-dose LA exposure (ANZCA).

options.text: Eclamptic seizure with secondary cardiopulmonary cardiac arrest
options.rationale: No history of hypertension, proteinuria, or seizures preceding collapse; presentation is hypoxic collapse with coagulopathy not seizure. Eclamptic seizure is correct with PE history and seizure activity (SOMANZ).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Acute pulmonary embolism with right heart failure
options.rationale: PE is differential but does not classically cause early DIC and oozing from IV sites; AFE more consistent with full picture. PE is correct in unilateral chest pain or DVT history (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium

options.text: Acute coronary syndrome with cardiogenic shock and arrest
options.rationale: ACS is possible but rare in a 36-year-old without risk factors and does not explain coagulopathy or temporal relationship with placental delivery. ACS is correct in older women with cardiac risk factors (RANZCOG).
source_refs: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium

explanation.summary: Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is a rare (~2-8 per 100,000 births) but devastating cause of maternal collapse. Mechanism: foetal amniotic constituents (squames, lanugo, vernix, trophoblast-derived tissue factor) breach the maternal venous compartment, triggering a biphasic insult - acute pulmonary vasoconstriction with right-heart failure producing hypoxia and hypotension, followed by a profound consumptive coagulopathy from tissue-factor-driven DIC and hyperfibrinolysis. The cluster of peripartum hypoxia, hypotension and DIC is therefore not coincidence; each element is a direct consequence of the same triggering embolic-immunological event. Diagnosis is clinical (Clark criteria); management is high-quality CPR with manual left uterine displacement (LUD), A-OK (atropine 1 mg, ondansetron 8 mg, ketorolac 30 mg IV per the AFE Foundation protocol), early massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 RBC:FFP:platelets), and perimortem caesarean by 4 minutes if no ROSC.
key_points: Clark criteria (4 required): cardiovascular collapse OR hypotension+respiratory compromise, DIC, intrapartum or within 30 min of placental delivery, no fever
Manual left uterine displacement throughout CPR (not tilt)
Perimortem CS by 4 minutes (4-minute rule) if no ROSC and >=20 weeks
Massive transfusion protocol; expect early cryoprecipitate for low fibrinogen
Differential of obstetric collapse: AFE, PE, eclampsia, MI, anaphylaxis, LAST, intracranial bleed, hypovolaemia
explanation.pearls: If the mother collapses with the placenta still in the room and starts oozing from her IV site, do not wait for confirmation - call MET and run the AFE algorithm now.

sources.label: RANZCOG: Maternal collapse in pregnancy and the puerperium
Clark SL et al. Proposed diagnostic criteria for AFE 2016

subtopic_detail: Maternal collapse in labour ward - amniotic fluid embolism

stem: A 33-year-old G1P0 at 41+5 is undergoing induction of labour for prolonged pregnancy. She has received two doses of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel 6 hours apart, the second 8 hours ago. Cervix today: 1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, head at -3 (Bishop score 3). No regular contractions. CTG reassuring, membranes intact. EFW 3.4 kg, no contraindications to mechanical methods, no previous caesarean, no antepartum haemorrhage, no foetal compromise. Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses of dinoprostone gel in 24 hours and prefers mechanical ripening when prostaglandins fail. She has no allergies.

data_table.Vital signs: Maternal BP 116/72 mmHg, HR 84 bpm, T 36.8 degC.
data_table.Examination: Bishop score 3 (1 cm long, posterior, firm, 1 cm dilated, -3 station). Membranes intact.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Insert a transcervical Foley catheter (16 Fr) with balloon inflated to 30-60 mL for up to 24 hours for mechanical cervical ripening
options.rationale: After failure of prostaglandins at maximum local dose with an unfavourable cervix, mechanical methods (Foley catheter or balloon) are the recommended next step. They are safe, effective and avoid hyperstimulation; ARM with oxytocin is inappropriate at Bishop 3 (RANZCOG IOL; NICE).

options.text: Administer a third dose of dinoprostone 2 mg vaginal gel today
options.rationale: Local protocol permits maximum 2 doses dinoprostone in 24 hours; exceeding risks hyperstimulation. Third dose is essentially never correct (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform artificial rupture of membranes and commence IV oxytocin infusion
options.rationale: ARM at Bishop 3 with high station is technically difficult and carries cord prolapse risk; cervix must first be ripened. ARM is correct when Bishop favourable (RANZCOG).

options.text: Perform a membrane sweep at the bedside and reassess in 24 hours
options.rationale: Membrane sweep is for low-Bishop cervices >=1 cm dilated, but insufficient as single intervention after failed prostaglandins. Sweep is correct in latent labour with favourable cervix (RANZCOG).

options.text: Proceed directly to category 4 elective caesarean section for failed induction of labour
options.rationale: Caesarean is for genuine failed induction after both pharmacological and mechanical methods; she has not failed mechanical. CS is correct after both methods exhausted (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: When the cervix remains unfavourable (Bishop <=6) after the maximum dose of prostaglandins, the next step is mechanical cervical ripening with a transcervical Foley catheter (or double balloon Cook catheter). This is comparable in efficacy to repeat prostaglandins with less tachysystole and is preferred for women with a previous CS. Caesarean for failed induction is reserved for women who fail mechanical ripening or who are not in active labour after ARM and oxytocin.
key_points: Bishop score: dilatation, length/effacement, station, position, consistency (max 13)
Bishop <=6 = unfavourable cervix; mechanical or pharmacological ripening required
Dinoprostone max 2 doses (Australian protocols)
Foley catheter 16 Fr with 30-60 mL balloon, leave 12-24 h or until expelled
Failed induction diagnosed only after ARM + adequate oxytocin trial without progress
explanation.pearls: Mechanical methods are particularly useful for women with a previous CS because they avoid the increased rupture risk of prostaglandins.

sources.label: KEMH Clinical Guideline: Induction of labour
Cochrane review: Mechanical methods for induction 2012

subtopic_detail: Failed induction - unfavourable cervix after prostaglandins

stem:
A 38-year-old G5P4 has had a postpartum haemorrhage after spontaneous vaginal birth of a 4.2 kg neonate following oxytocin augmentation. Despite 40 IU oxytocin infusion, IM ergometrine, three doses IM carboprost, IV tranexamic acid 1 g, and a Bakri balloon at 500 mL, she continues to bleed. Total EBL 3500 mL. BP 88/52, HR 134, peripheries cool. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L, platelets 68.

MTP active, packed cells/FFP/platelets running. She has completed her family. Consultant obstetrician is at the bedside, gynae-oncology is in-house, interventional radiology is on site, anaesthetic team is in theatre.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 88/52 mmHg, HR 134 bpm, T 36.0 degC.
data_table.Examination: Atonic uterus despite Bakri 500 mL. Cool peripheries. In theatre, laparotomy imminent.
data_table.Investigations: Total EBL 3500 mL. Fibrinogen 1.4 g/L. Platelets 68 (x10^9/L). Massive transfusion protocol active.

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Proceed to laparotomy with uterine-sparing measures first (B-Lynch compression suture +/- stepwise uterine artery ligation), escalating to peripartum hysterectomy if bleeding persists
options.rationale: RANZCOG PPH algorithm: after failure of medical therapy and balloon tamponade, the recommended surgical sequence is conservative interventions (B-Lynch compression, uterine artery ligation) before hysterectomy. She has completed her family so hysterectomy is acceptable if conservative steps fail, but should not be the first surgical step (RANZCOG PPH).

options.text: Proceed directly to peripartum hysterectomy without trialling uterine-sparing surgery
options.rationale: Hysterectomy is last resort even with completed family; conservative surgical measures recommended first if stabilisable. Direct hysterectomy is correct in life-threatening haemorrhage when uterine-sparing measures cannot be performed safely (RANZCOG).

options.text: Insert a second balloon alongside the Bakri and arrange transfer to interventional radiology for embolisation
options.rationale: Haemodynamically unstable; transfer to IR outside theatre is unsafe. IR is correct in stable patients (RANZCOG).

options.text: Re-inflate Bakri to 750 mL and observe for 30 minutes before further intervention
options.rationale: Bakri has maximum fill ~500 mL; overinflation does not increase efficacy. Observation in shock is unsafe. Re-inflation is essentially never correct in failed Bakri (RANZCOG).

options.text: Bilateral internal iliac artery ligation as the first surgical step
options.rationale: Internal iliac artery ligation is technically demanding, takes time, and reserved for after uterine artery ligation or by IR/vascular if available. Internal iliac is correct after B-Lynch and uterine artery ligation fail (RANZCOG).

explanation.summary: Surgical escalation for intractable atonic PPH after failed medical and balloon therapy: (1) compression suture such as B-Lynch, (2) stepwise uterine devascularisation (uterine artery ligation, then ovarian artery ligation), (3) internal iliac artery ligation or interventional radiology embolisation, (4) peripartum hysterectomy as last resort. Hysterectomy is performed earlier if the patient is unstable, the uterus is severely damaged, or in placenta accreta spectrum.
key_points: Uterine-sparing options first unless contraindication
B-Lynch suture: vertical compression, leaves uterine cavity accessible
Stepwise uterine devascularisation: uterine arteries then ovarian arteries
Internal iliac (hypogastric) artery ligation: technically demanding
Peripartum hysterectomy: last resort, but do not delay if unstable
Always involve a senior obstetrician early; consider IR if available and patient stable enough
explanation.pearls: The clock matters: do not let pride in uterine-sparing surgery delay hysterectomy in an exsanguinating patient. The right operation is the one that stops the bleeding before the patient dies.

sources.label: Royal Women's Hospital Postpartum haemorrhage clinical guideline
